title: THORChain deposit credit validation bypass historical bridge incident

historical_exploit.attack_summary: The decentralized cross-chain transaction protocol THORChain (RUNE) updated the attack situation, claiming that the amount of lost assets was about 4000 ETH. The initial assessment is that the attack was a logical vulnerability when Eth Bifrost used the routing contract to capture ERC-20 tokens. The attacker use. Not long ago, THORChain updated Eth Bifrost to allow the routing contract to be "encapsulated" by the contract. The attacker uses this to send a transaction with msg.value = 200 ETH and immediately uses the contract to transfer it back to itself, while Bifrost will report msg. value = 200 instead of depositAmount = 0, so as to realize the profit of calling the routing contract with the amount of 0 ETH.
historical_exploit.attacker_profile: External attacker or unprivileged caller as described by the SlowMist incident row; exact actor permissions must be re-verified before reporting.
preconditions: A bridge, cross-chain router, gateway, wrapped-asset minter, liquidity pool, or validator/proof path accepts attacker-controlled message, proof, signature, transaction, deposit, configuration, or accounting input.
The accepted input releases existing bridge-held value, mints unbacked wrapped assets, miscredits a deposit, or lets a caller move assets beyond entitlement.
The target mapping must be reachable without relying on privileged key compromise, DNS/frontend phishing, social-account compromise, or pure rug-pull behavior.

value_path.asset_or_reward: bridge-held assets, wrapped assets, bridge liquidity, withdrawals, refunds, or backed cross-chain liabilities
value_path.how_value_was_extracted: The decentralized cross-chain transaction protocol THORChain (RUNE) updated the attack situation, claiming that the amount of lost assets was about 4000 ETH. The initial assessment is that the attack was a logical vulnerability when Eth Bifrost used the routing contract to capture ERC-20 tokens. The attacker use. Not long ago, THORChain updated Eth Bifrost to allow the routing contract to be "encapsulated" by the contract. The attacker uses this to send a transaction with msg.value = 200 ETH and immediately uses the contract to transfer it back to itself, while Bifrost will report msg. value = 200 instead of depositAmount = 0, so as to realize the profit of calling the routing contract with the amount of 0 ETH.
value_path.why_it_paid: The historical row describes real loss, attempted bridge theft, unbacked minting, unauthorized withdrawal, or bridge-accounting failure that can be useful only if BridgeIndex has a matching current live-value extraction path.

mapping.why_this_might_map: BridgeIndex exposes bridge deposits, bot-gas accounting, withdrawal creation/approval/execution, refunds, token/resource mappings, relayer voting, and proposal execution. This historical primitive is useful only if it maps to extracting current BNB/USDT ledger value or backed bridge liabilities from the deployed BridgeIndex system.

notes: Selected for scanned because: THORChain false top-up / deposit-credit logic failure
This seed is not proof that BridgeIndex is vulnerable; Workflow 7 must reject it unless the primitive maps to non-admin extraction of a current live ledger row.
